criteria: The agent has read Section III.A to understand the overall U-Net-like architecture of nnFormer consisting of encoder (embedding + two local transformer blocks + two down-sampling layers), bottleneck (three global transformer blocks), and decoder (two transformer blocks + two up-sampling layers + patch expanding layer).
The agent has read Section III.B to understand the encoder's convolutional embedding block with four successive convolutional layers of kernel size 3 followed by GELU and layer normalization.
The agent has read Equation 1 to understand the two-layer LV-MSA transformer block computation with residual connections and MLP.
The agent has read Equation 3 to understand the QKV attention formulation with relative position encoding B.
The agent has read Equations 2 and 4 to understand the computational complexity differences between LV-MSA and GV-MSA, motivating their placement in the encoder vs. bottleneck.

The agent has read Section III.C to understand the bottleneck design using GV-MSA (global volume-based multi-head self-attention) with three global transformer blocks providing large receptive fields.
The agent has read Section III.D and Equations 5-6 to understand the skip attention mechanism that replaces concatenation/summation in skip connections.

The agent has read Section IV.A completely to understand all implementation details: initial LR=0.01, poly decay with exponent 0.9, SGD momentum=0.99, weight decay=3e-5, 1000 epochs of 250 iterations each, and attention head configurations.
The agent has read Equation 7 to understand the polynomial learning rate decay schedule.
The agent has read Equation 8 to understand the deep supervision loss formulation at three resolutions with halved magnitude factors.
The agent has read Table I to understand the dataset-specific network configurations: spacing, median shape, crop size, batch size, and down-sampling strides for Tumor, Synapse, and ACDC.
The agent has read Table II to understand the brain tumor segmentation comparison against transformer-based baselines using HD95 and DSC on WT, ET, and TC sub-regions.
The agent has read Table III to understand the Synapse multi-organ segmentation comparison against transformer-based methods with per-organ DSC scores for 8 abdominal organs.
The agent has read Table IV to understand the ACDC automated cardiac diagnosis comparison using DSC for RV, Myo, and LV.
The agent has read Table V to understand the detailed comparison between nnFormer and nnUNet on all three datasets, including per-class results and the nnAvg model ensemble.
The agent has read Table VI to understand the six-variant ablation study investigating the contribution of Conv. Embed., Conv. Down., GV-MSA, Skip Attention, and SLV-MSA on ACDC.

The plan includes the overall nnFormer U-Net-like architecture: encoder with one embedding layer, two local transformer blocks, and two down-sampling layers; bottleneck with one down-sampling, three global transformer blocks, and one up-sampling; decoder with two transformer blocks, two up-sampling layers, and one patch expanding layer.
The plan includes implementing the convolutional embedding block with four successive convolutional layers of kernel size 3, GELU activation, and layer normalization after each layer except the last.
The plan includes implementing LV-MSA and SLV-MSA as specified in Equation 1, with two successive transformer layers per block and shifted volumes in SLV-MSA.
The plan includes implementing QKV attention with relative position encoding B as specified in Equation 3, including initialization of smaller position matrix B̂.
The plan includes implementing GV-MSA for the bottleneck with three global transformer blocks (six GV-MSA layers total) to provide large receptive fields.
The plan includes implementing the skip attention mechanism (Equations 5 and 6) to replace traditional concatenation/summation in skip connections.
The plan includes implementing convolutional down-sampling with dataset-specific strides and strided deconvolution for up-sampling.
The plan includes implementing deep supervision at three resolutions as per Equation 8 with magnitude factors α₁, α₂=α₁/2, α₃=α₁/4 normalized to 1.
The plan includes all training hyperparameters: initial LR=0.01, poly decay with exponent 0.9 (Equation 7), SGD with momentum=0.99 and weight decay=3e-5, 1000 epochs of 250 iterations each.
The plan includes using combined cross entropy and dice loss by simple summation.

The plan includes data preprocessing (resampling to target spacing per dataset) and the augmentation pipeline of rotation, scaling, gaussian noise, gaussian blur, brightness, contrast, low resolution simulation, gamma augmentation, and mirroring.

The plan includes experiments on all three datasets: Brain Tumor MSD (484 MRI, 4-channel, 80/15/5 split), Synapse (30 CT, 18/12 split, 8 organs), and ACDC (100 patients, 70/10/20 split, 3 structures).
The plan includes all six ablation study variants from Table VI, progressively adding Conv. Embed., Conv. Down., GV-MSA, Skip Attention, and SLV-MSA.
The plan includes repeating each experiment 10 times and averaging results for statistical significance testing with p-values.
The plan includes model ensembling by averaging predictions of nnFormer and nnUNet (nnAvg) on all three datasets.
The code for the overall nnFormer U-Net-like architecture has been implemented with encoder, bottleneck, and decoder as three distinct modules with skip connections.

The code for the convolutional embedding block has been implemented with four successive convolutional layers of kernel size 3, GELU activation, and layer normalization after each layer except the last.

The code for LV-MSA has been implemented as Equation 1: input X_LV ∈ R^{L×C} is reshaped to R^{N_LV × N_T × C} where N_T = S_H × S_W × S_D, followed by local volume self-attention and MLP with residual connections.
The code for SLV-MSA has been implemented as the shifted version of LV-MSA, displacing local volumes by (⌊S_H/2⌋, ⌊S_W/2⌋, ⌊S_D/2⌋) to enable interactions between different local volumes.

The code for the local transformer block has been implemented with two successive transformer layers: first applying LV-MSA then SLV-MSA, each followed by MLP with residual connections as in Equation 1.
The code for QKV attention has been implemented as Equation 3: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k + B)V, using Q, K, V ∈ R^{N_T × d_k}.

The code for relative position encoding has been implemented by initializing a smaller position matrix B̂ ∈ R^{(2S_H-1)×(2S_W-1)×(2S_D-1)} and taking corresponding values to build the larger position matrix B.
The code for GV-MSA has been implemented as the 3D global multi-head self-attention operating on the entire feature volume (without local window partitioning) in the bottleneck.

The code for the bottleneck has been implemented with three global transformer blocks each containing two GV-MSA layers (six GV-MSA layers total), providing sufficient large receptive field for the decoder.
The code for Skip Attention linear projection has been implemented as Equation 5: LP(X_{LV,GV}^l) produces key matrix K^{l*} and value matrix V^{l*} from encoder features.

The code for Skip Attention attention computation has been implemented as Equation 6: using decoder up-sampled output X_UP^{l*} as query Q^{l*}, and encoder-projected K^{l*} and V^{l*} for cross-attention.

The code for convolutional down-sampling has been implemented using strided convolution with dataset-specific strides per dimension as specified in Table I (e.g., stride=1 in dimensions where the number of slices is limited).
The code for strided deconvolution up-sampling has been implemented in the decoder, symmetrically to the down-sampling layers.
The code for the final patch expanding layer has been implemented using deconvolution to produce final mask predictions from the decoder.
The code for multi-head self-attention has been implemented with the correct number of heads per encoder stage: [6, 12, 24, 48] for Synapse and [3, 6, 12, 24] for Brain Tumor and ACDC.
The code for MLP in transformer blocks has been implemented as a two-layer neural network (as noted in Figure 3 caption).
The code for layer normalization has been implemented before both attention and MLP operations in each transformer layer.
The code for deep supervision has been implemented as Equation 8: summing losses at three resolutions {H,W,D}, {H/4,W/4,D/2}, {H/8,W/8,D/4} weighted by α₁, α₂, α₃.
The code for deep supervision magnitude factors has been implemented with α₂ = α₁/2 and α₃ = α₁/4, with all factors normalized to sum to 1.
The code for the combined training loss has been implemented by simply summing cross entropy loss and dice loss.
The code for polynomial learning rate decay has been implemented as Equation 7: lr = initial_lr × (1 - epoch_id / max_epoch)^0.9 with initial_lr=0.01 and max_epoch=1000.
The code for the SGD optimizer has been implemented with momentum=0.99 and weight decay=3e-5.
The code for training duration has been implemented with 1000 epochs of 250 iterations each.
The code for data preprocessing has been implemented to resample all images to the target spacing: [1.0,1.0,1.0] for Tumor, [0.76,0.76,3] for Synapse, and [1.52,1.52,6.35] for ACDC.

The code for data augmentation has been implemented with all nine specified strategies applied in order: rotation, scaling, gaussian noise, gaussian blur, brightness adjustment, contrast adjustment, low resolution simulation, gamma augmentation, and mirroring.

The code for loading the Brain Tumor (MSD) dataset has been implemented, handling 484 MRI images with 4 channels (FLAIR, T1w, T1gd, T2w) and segmentation labels for three sub-regions (ED, ET, NET) with 80%/15%/5% train/val/test split.

The code for loading the Synapse multi-organ segmentation dataset has been implemented with 18 training cases and 12 test cases for segmenting 8 abdominal organs (aorta, gallbladder, spleen, left kidney, right kidney, liver, pancreas, stomach).
The code for loading the ACDC cardiac segmentation dataset has been implemented with 70 training, 10 validation, and 20 test patients segmenting three structures (LV, RV, MYO).
The network configuration for the Brain Tumor dataset has been implemented with crop size 128×128×128, batch size 2, and down-sampling strides [2,2,2]×5.
The network configuration for the Synapse dataset has been implemented with crop size 128×128×64, batch size 2, and down-sampling strides [2,2,2],[2,2,1],[2,2,2],[2,2,2],[2,2,2].
The network configuration for the ACDC dataset has been implemented with crop size 160×160×14, batch size 4, and down-sampling strides [2,2,1],[2,2,1],[2,2,1],[2,2,2],[2,2,2],[2,2,2].

The code for two local transformer blocks in the encoder has been implemented, each containing two successive transformer layers (LV-MSA and SLV-MSA), with two convolutional down-sampling layers between and after them.
The code for two transformer blocks in the decoder has been implemented symmetrically to the encoder, with skip attention connections from corresponding encoder stages.
The code for evaluation metrics has been implemented: 95th percentile Hausdorff Distance (HD95) and Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC).
The code for the ablation study baseline (Table VI line 0) has been implemented with 1×LV-MSA (without SLV-MSA), patch merging (PM) and patch embedding (PE) from Swin Transformer.

The code for all six ablation variants in Table VI has been implemented, progressively substituting and adding Conv. Embed. (line 1), Conv. Down. (line 2), GV-MSA (line 3), Skip Attention (line 4), and SLV-MSA with doubled GV-MSA (line 5).
The code for model ensembling (nnAvg) has been implemented by averaging output predictions from nnFormer and nnUNet.
The code for nnFormer training and inference on the Brain Tumor (MSD) dataset has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for nnFormer training and inference on the Synapse multi-organ segmentation dataset has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for nnFormer training and inference on the ACDC cardiac segmentation dataset has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for all six ablation study variants on the ACDC dataset has been executed successfully without errors.
Each experiment has been repeated 10 times with average results computed for reporting.
The model ensemble (nnAvg) combining predictions from nnFormer and nnUNet has been executed on all three datasets.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table II: nnFormer achieves average HD95=4.05 mm and DSC=86.4% on brain tumor segmentation, surpassing all transformer-based baselines including UNETR (HD95=8.82, DSC=71.1%).
The execution outcomes match the per-class results in Table II: WT (HD95=3.80, DSC=91.3%), ET (HD95=3.87, DSC=81.8%), TC (HD95=4.49, DSC=86.0%).
The execution outcomes match the results in Table III: nnFormer achieves average HD95=10.63 mm and DSC=86.57% on Synapse multi-organ segmentation, outperforming all transformer baselines.

The execution outcomes match the per-organ DSC results in Table III: aorta=92.04%, gallbladder=70.17%, kidney (left)=86.57%, kidney (right)=86.25%, liver=96.84%, pancreas=83.35%, spleen=90.51%, stomach=86.83%.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table IV: nnFormer achieves average DSC=92.06% on ACDC (RV=90.94%, Myo=89.58%, LV=95.65%), outperforming all transformer-based baselines including LeViT-UNet-384s (DSC=90.32%).
The execution outcomes match the results in Table V: nnFormer achieves lower average HD95 than nnUNet on all three datasets (Tumor: 4.42 vs 4.60, Synapse: 10.63 vs 10.78, ACDC: 1.12 vs 1.15).
The execution outcomes match the nnAvg results in Table V: model ensembling improves over both individual models, especially on Synapse HD95 (nnAvg=7.70 vs nnFormer=10.63 vs nnUNet=10.78).
The execution outcomes match the ablation results in Table VI: the baseline (line 0) achieves DSC=90.55% and the full nnFormer (line 5) achieves DSC=92.06% on ACDC.

The execution outcomes match the progressive improvements in Table VI: Conv. Embed. (+0.4%), Conv. Down. (+0.3%), GV-MSA (+0.2%), Skip Att. (+0.4%), SLV-MSA (+0.2%), each contributing incrementally to the final DSC.
The execution outcomes confirm p-values less than 1e-2 for HD95 comparisons between nnFormer and the best transformer-based baseline on all three datasets.
